original_hypothesis.title: Forwarder verify() and execute() Have Inconsistent Validation Allowing Misleading Off-Chain Checks
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Forwarder.verify() does NOT check req.to == _eurfAddress, while execute() does. A request that passes verify() off-chain may fail execute() on-chain. This inconsistency can mislead relayers into submitting transactions that will revert, wasting gas without receiving the gasless base fee.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Relayers use verify() to pre-validate requests before spending gas on execute(). The missing req.to check in verify() creates a false positive that leads to wasted gas when execute() reverts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: verify() checks nonce, sig, and selector but NOT req.to == _eurfAddress; execute() checks all four

preconditions: A ForwardRequest has req.to != _eurfAddress but valid signature and correct selector
Relayer uses verify() to pre-check before calling execute()

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: Forwarder.sol lines 60-72 (verify) missing req.to check present at line 87 (execute)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Asymmetric validation between verify() and execute()
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: verify() returns success for requests that execute() will revert on

analysis_questions: Do production relayers rely on verify() before calling execute()?
Can this be used to grief relayers into wasting significant gas?
Are there other validation differences between verify() and execute()?
validation_requirements: Test: construct request with wrong req.to, call verify (passes), call execute (reverts)
Quantify gas wasted per griefing attempt
Check if relayer fee (gaslessBasefee) is lost on revert

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker signs a valid ForwardRequest with req.to set to a non-EURF address. Relayer calls verify() which passes all checks. Relayer calls execute() which reverts at require(req.to == _eurfAddress). Relayer loses gas cost. The gaslessBasefee is never paid since execute() reverts entirely.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Relayer gas griefing; no token loss but operational cost to relayers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Relayers can add off-chain req.to check

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing verify() and execute() validation paths

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via repeated revert patterns from same address

validation_result.mechanism: asymmetric validation between verify() and execute()
validation_result.impact_summary: Forwarder.verify() omits the req.to == _eurfAddress check present in execute(), causing false positives that mislead relayers into submitting transactions that will revert, wasting gas.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The Forwarder contract provides two key functions: verify() for off-chain pre-validation and execute() for on-chain execution. These functions should perform identical validation checks (minus state changes), but they don't. execute() at line ~93 includes `require(req.to == _eurfAddress, ...)` which is completely absent from verify(). This means a ForwardRequest with req.to set to any address other than _eurfAddress will pass verify() but revert in execute(). This is a clear code defect in the verify() function - it fails to fulfill its stated purpose of pre-validating requests.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct comparison of the two functions confirms the asymmetry. verify() (lines ~60-76) checks: _verifyNonce, _verifySig, and selector == transfer. execute() (lines ~78-106) checks: _verifyNonce, _verifySig, req.to == _eurfAddress, and selector == transfer. The req.to check is present only in execute().

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: verify() is missing `require(req.to == _eurfAddress, ...)` which is present in execute(). A request with req.to != _eurfAddress passes verify() but reverts in execute().
execute() contains the req.to == _eurfAddress check at line ~93 that verify() lacks, creating the validation asymmetry.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker creates ForwardRequest with req.to = arbitrary_address (not _eurfAddress), valid signature, correct nonce, transfer selector
2. Relayer calls Forwarder.verify(req, ...) off-chain or via static call
4. verify() calls _verifySig(req, ...) - passes (valid signature from req.from)
7. Relayer, trusting verify(), submits execute(req, ...) on-chain
12. Entire transaction reverts, relayer loses gas, nonce increment is rolled back
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical divergence is between verify() at line ~60-76 which lacks the req.to check, and execute() at line ~93 which requires req.to == _eurfAddress. A relayer relying on verify() for pre-validation will get a false positive for any request where req.to != _eurfAddress.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts for Forwarder provided, but the defect is purely in the contract code logic, independent of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward: an attacker signs a ForwardRequest with req.to set to any address other than _eurfAddress (e.g., another ERC20 token or any random address), with a valid signature, correct nonce, and transfer selector in req.data. The relayer calls verify() which passes all checks. The relayer then submits execute() which reverts at the req.to check. The relayer loses gas costs for the failed transaction. The attacker bears zero cost since signing a message is free. This can be repeated with incrementing nonces (though nonces don't actually increment on revert, the attacker could use the same nonce repeatedly). Note: since execute() reverts entirely, the nonce is NOT consumed, meaning the attacker can grief indefinitely with the same signed message until the relayer adds off-chain filtering.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium severity. The defect enables relayer gas griefing at zero cost to the attacker. Relayers that rely on verify() for pre-validation will waste gas on transactions that are guaranteed to revert. The gaslessBasefee is never paid since the entire transaction reverts. While this doesn't result in token loss, it can cause operational costs and potential denial of service for the relayer infrastructure. The attack is repeatable since the nonce isn't consumed on revert.
recommended_mitigations: Add `require(req.to == _eurfAddress, "NGEUR Forwarder: can only forward NGEUR transactions");` to the verify() function, matching the check in execute()
Alternatively, refactor to extract a shared internal _validate() function called by both verify() and execute() to ensure validation parity
Relayers should add off-chain req.to validation as an immediate mitigation
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Forwarder and EURFToken. 2. Initialize Forwarder with EURFToken address. 3. Sign a ForwardRequest with req.to = some_other_address (not EURFToken), valid nonce=0, transfer selector. 4. Call verify() via staticcall - confirm it succeeds. 5. Call execute() - confirm it reverts with 'can only forward NGEUR transactions'. 6. Measure gas consumed on the reverted execute() call.
uncertainties: Whether production relayers actually use verify() for pre-validation or have their own off-chain checks
The exact gas cost per griefing attempt depends on the EVM gas schedule and call parameters
additional_testing_needed: Test verify() with req.to != _eurfAddress to confirm it passes
Test execute() with same request to confirm it reverts
Measure gas consumed on the reverted execute() to quantify griefing cost
Check if relayer implementation uses verify() in its workflow
